sentences.text: My name is Elsa Chandler, spelled E-L-S-A, but in Europe, it was spelled with a Z.
My maiden name was Biller, B-I- double-L-E-R.
I was born in Lwow, or called, from time to time, Lemberg in Poland.
And I was born in, on August the 2nd, 1932.
I can't exactly tell you from what time on it was.
But I have certain memories, obviously.
I can remember living in an apartment in a building where there was a very interesting elevator that had all kinds of carvings on it.
And it was supposedly a new contraption, because people came to look at it.
And I had a nanny.
And there was a maid in the house.
And I had a grandmother living in the same city in Lwow.
I also, from time to time, used to go to a place called Brody, a small, a small, I guess you would call it a town, not very far from Lwow.
It was also in Galicia.
And I used to visit my maternal grandparents there and the family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Usually, I would go with someone on a train there and spend some time.
And usually, I think it was a nanny.
And I remember some vivid memories of that.
But I can't tell you how old I was.
My grandparents had what was called a Druckerei, which is a, a print shop.
And that was very interesting with all the machinery going.
And I'm, I'm quite sure that by today's standards, it was ancient.
But in those days, to hear the noise of, of the machines going was very exciting for a child.
And I would peek in.
And always-- I was always told not to touch.
They also had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because it was a small town, and I think they lived on the outskirts and had sort of a big home with dogs that used to bark.
And I was told not to go close to them because they were vicious.
And there was, there were a couple of cows and some animals.
So that was very exciting for a child.
In fact, I was not too long ago telling my grandchildren that in the attic, there was a huge box of buttons.
And some of them came from military, military wear.
And they were very interesting.
And I also have a box of buttons which I keep for the kids to play with.
So I was telling them that I remembered those kind of things.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember my mother more vividly then I remember the rest of the family.
Of course, I was with her the longest, until the end of war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We spent summers holidaying in different places.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In fact, I think I showed you some pictures of that, where I was with my grandparents and my parents in different places.
I can't exactly tell you where.
I know one was Zakopane, and there were some, some German resorts that we used to go to in the summertime.
I don't really remember going to school.
You know, many times I think I must have gone to school, because when the war ended, I did know a little bit how to write.
So I must have gone to school, but I don't really remember.
I can't remember if I had a tutor or if I did go to school.
I can't remember a class or anything like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not far from where we lived, there was a big hill.
And I remember tobogganing on it.
So those are the kind of memories that, that I have.
They're not extensive.
But, but they're good memories.
They're memories of, of a good life.
I-- my father was born in Lwow.
And he had several sisters.
I wouldn't be surprised if there were as many as five.
And he was the only brother.
His father died when he was young.
And I, again, don't know what that means, because, you know, it could be when he was a boy, or it could be when he was a teenager or a young man.
But I know from speaking to the aunt that lived in Canada when I came to Canada, that they all strived for him to get an education.
And in fact, he is the only one in the family that received a very good education.
And they came from an Orthodox family.
And I think it was not that common in the time for someone to go to university and get that kind of education that he did from an Orthodox family.
His name was-- they called him [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
And I believe it's, it's Isaac or Izrack, something like that.
But they called him [NON-ENGLISH].
My mother, on the other hand, was born in Brody.
And Brody at the time was under the Austrians.
And in fact, they considered themselves of the Deutsche Kultur.
And there, like in most of Galicia, education and culture and all kinds of things that had to do with theater and music and all this-- it was a revival of the intelligentsia there.
And so my mother and my mother's brother went to school, obviously.
And then they both studied in Vienna as well.
So that's the kind of things that I know.
And I also remember, and I also remember that even after my father was already working, when he went to the university in Lwow for a little while, I, I, I heard discussion about Jews being beat up.
And, and there were some very bad situations.
And that must have been, if I remember, it must have been already in 1938 or something like even 1939, just prior to the Russians coming into Lwow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a little brother who was four years younger.
And his name was Julias.
And there were only my brother and myself.
There were no other siblings.
Well, I think that my life changed in 1941, when the Germans came into Poland.
But that doesn't mean that life didn't change before.
Because in 1939, the Russians came into, into Lwow, into the eastern part of, of Europe.
And I know that life changed for my family.
For me, life did not change.

In fact, life was very good at that time, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother-- I'm not sure about my father, but my mother was fairly fluent in French, because that was the second language that one learned in Poland.
My mother was fluent in Russian.
My mother spoke German with, with the kind of fluency that one speaks who's born to the language and spoke Polish.
And somehow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were, there were-- the people who were in charge befriended my family.
For what purpose, I don't know.
But the Russian people who came into Lwow were very close with my family.
And life was normal for us.
In fact, when some of these people ran back to Russia, when the Germans entered Lwow, they came in the middle of the night and begged us to go back with them to Russia.
But of course, my father felt-- and particularly my mother-- that under German, German rule, it would never be bad.

Because she truly believed that after all, a nation such as, such well, such well-educated nation, a nation of such stature, could not be any worse than some of the people who were so backward as in Russia.
So it was very interesting.
In '41, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quite-- I can't tell you timewise, because it's sort of a little difficult--
--for me to, to put the clock on, on things and the time.

But I think that was in the first week or two they gathered-- the Germans, the Gestapo gathered-- all the intelligentsia-- and particularly, not only, not only Jewish intelligentsia, but Polish intelligentsia, too.
They came.
They took my father away.
And we've never seen him again.
Now, people are asking me, don't you know if he died?
Don't you know what happened to him?
Did they send him someplace?
We don't know.

But the presumption was, since we have never heard from him again-- and I'm sure that my mother at that time must have made an awful lot of inquiries, because she had a lot of connections-- that they must have killed these people.
Where and how, I couldn't tell you.
Yeah, no, no, no.
At this point, I'm nine years old.
I'm nine years old.
I'm nine years old, and I come from a family where I wasn't exactly a child that was exposed to a lot of life reality.
I come from a family where there were only two children.
And my father was-- had a good position.
And my mother came from a family where there was also a lot of help in the house.
So I was a sheltered child.
And I guess [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think that my, my mother sheltered me an awful lot as well.
Then what happened-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Jews in Lwow had to go into the Jewish quarter.
And we went into the Jewish ghetto.
And within the Jewish ghetto area, my paternal grandmother lived there, because she lived in the Jewish area.
And we lived outside of the Jewish area.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There, already, we were in a one-room.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother was all the time hidden.
And it was my responsibility to watch over him.
He was, well, he was four years younger, so five, five, four.
And I don't know his birth date, so it depends if he was four or five.
I think that there was always fear that little children might cry and might upset somebody.
And I was pretty well sort of watched over, and a little bit not quite hidden, but not exposed, either.
There was always a fear that you are going to get it over the head with the bayonet or, or the rifle and stuff like that.
There was a tremendous amount of cruelty, a tremendous amount of cruelty.
I have a feeling that in the beginning of the war, even, even more so, maybe, than in the end, some of the people that, that were sent into Poland were very young, the soldiers.
And I don't know, they are-- young people sometimes, especially if they are taught to hate, they know what to do to people that they hate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we were in that ghetto for a while.
And after a time, my grandmother was no longer with us.
And I cannot remember what happened to her.
I cannot remember.
And whenever I try to think, it's like as though she disappeared.
And then my brother disappeared.
And so what I know now is that the little children were actually taken away.
And there was a camp near Poland called Janowska.
And they just put the little children in an open grave, and they burned them alive.
And so around that time, by the time-- 1991, 1992-- 1991?
I mean 1941, 1942, there was only my mother and myself left.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were in Lwow for a little while.
But I think that my mother felt we were just the only two left.
And I'm sure that she was extremely concerned about my well-being.
And she was also concerned that there was no longer family there.
So we had a very influential uncle who lived in Krakow.
His name was Ephraim Ratz.
I, I hear that it's a well-known name prior to the war in Krakow.
And he had a factory, and he was quite influential.
And she must have been in touch with him.
And they brought us to Krakow.
How they brought us-- again, I don't remember.
I don't remember if we went by horse and buggy or if we went by car or if we went by train.
But we did end up in the Krakow ghetto.
And at that time, he was already in the ghetto.
Yes, I, I did sense a difference.
And the difference was, I think, that my uncle had a little better quarters.
It wasn't like a one-room.
It was still a little more livable.
And then there was-- it wasn't my uncle, but it was my mother's uncle.
And my mother's uncle was there and his wife.
And there were more people around that I think my mother was familiar with.
So there was a more-- the, the family was a little more extensive.
And there was a little more security for me in, in the ghetto than when I was in Lwow.
Because there, I was left by myself, and my mother wasn't with me most of the time.
And one of the reasons that she wasn't with me was that they used her as a, as a dolmetscher-- as a, as a translator or interpreter.
And so I guess her services were needed.
And, and that provided us with some security.
Well, there was certainly no school.
And I don't remember playing.
I do remember being taught.
I do remember my mother and my uncle trying to teach me.
And the little bits of books and papers that were available were extremely limited.
But education was extremely important in, in that family.
And, and they were living with hope that the war would end.
And, and how would I ever amount to anything?
And that was very important.
So I remember a little bit of that.
I remember some things that are just insignificant.
And yet when you think today, especially with my involvement in education, that my mother taught me the piano scales in concentration camp on a piece of paper.
Well, a little piece of paper, and you have the scales, and you say this is this, and this is how you move your fingers.
And it was very interesting.
Because when my children took piano lessons, many years ago, and we had a teacher from the Conservatory of Music come to the house, because I had three of them taking lessons.
So she said to me one day, why don't you, too?
And I said, you know, I, I would like to.
I can't really afford it.
And because it was the days when I really couldn't even afford to provide my children with the lessons.
But she was amazed that there were things I remembered.
Again, time is difficult to put a finger on.
But I would say a good few months.
We-- I was in the ghetto from about 1941, end '41, '42, until the ghetto was liquidated.
And I mean, I could look up in the book to see when the ghetto was liquidated.
But I don't think you're after these kind of dates.
But when the ghetto was liquidated, people were taken to Plaszow concentration camp.
And that's when I ended up in Plaszow.
When I sometimes talk to my husband about it, he says to me my memory is a bit confused.
But they took the people into Plaszow.
And slowly, they took out all the people.
But they did not take the children.
Plaszow supposedly was a, was a camp where there were no children.
But there were a few children in Plaszow-- very few, but a few.
And I was one of them.
Plaszow was a labor camp.
Also in Plaszow they had a camp for some Polish prisoners of war.
It was sort of a mixed kind of a, a camp.
And when they liquidated the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the children were left behind, I was left behind, too.
And I remember in the middle of the night-- and I'm quite sure that my uncle must have given half of Krakow away for that-- people came looking for me.
And I heard people yelling and calling my name.
And I was taken to Plaszow in the middle of the night.
And I believe that I also know that there were some fires in the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My husband says to me, the ghetto did not burn.
But I am saying that there were fires in the ghetto.
I, I wish sometimes-- you know, I've been so busy with my life, making a, making a new life for myself, that I haven't, maybe, dwelled enough on it.
In fact, it's only in the last couple of years that I've been talking about it.
My husband has been talking to the children about it since they were little.
But I never said anything.
And, and it's been difficult.
It's, it's difficult to recall, especially when you've tried to forget.
Well, in Plaszow, I was mostly hidden-- and again, on the bunks, hidden behind whatever in darkened part of, of, of the camp.
And life was very difficult.
I don't remember horrible hunger in Plaszow.
I don't remember that.
But I remember horrible fear.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the hunger started much later, and the sickness, of course.
But I don't remember hunger in Plaszow for myself.
And my mother, again, was, was a needed entity, because they needed her knowledge in, in, in the languages.
And, and I guess they needed, maybe, her other skills that she had, which were skills in writing and, and speaking and whatever.
After Plaszow, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after Plaszow, it was already-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went with the transports when they were sending the transports.
And my mother and I went to, to Skarzysko-Kamienna where they had an ammunition factory, and from there to Tschenstochau, Czestochowa.
And those were camps, labor camps.
They made ammunitions in those.
Skarzysko-Kamienna, they, they made bullets, I think.
In, in fact, some people would get sort of a yellow color to them from the powders that were used, et cetera.
And in those camps, in one of those camps, I was cleaning a Gestapo's shoes, or I don't know if he was a Gestapo or if he wasn't, but he was a soldier, et cetera.

And I-- sometimes, from time to time, I remember him giving me a very beautifully thin kind of sandwich that they had, these little sandwiches that were very elegant, which I wasn't familiar with [LAUGHS] at the time.
In fact, I don't ever-- I'm never familiar with thin sandwiches, since I always have thick sandwiches.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But in those days in the other camps, I was very much protected by my mother, so as to make sure that, that I lived.
Because there was no place to hide.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was hunger.
And there was fear.
And there were selections.
And people died.
And, and one never knew from day to day what the day would hold.
And it depended on what kind of commandant came into the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So it was very hard, very hard.
I, many times now, when I think in retrospect, I don't think I feared.
I don't think that there were fears.
I think that there was just a matter of surviving from day to day.
It wasn't, it wasn't like you're thinking of what you're going to do today.
It was whether you're going to survive today.
And, and I think you saw so much death around one that I don't even know for sure if one was really scared of death.
I think sometimes one was more scared of living.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think people were more scared of being beat up and hungry than of death.
Well, from these camps, I ended up with--
